core.intro: Wherever it is warm enough, wax jambu is grown the same way: give it heat, humidity, a frost-free spot and a reliable supply of water, and it rewards you with crisp, glossy, bell-shaped fruit, often several crops a year. The state sections below cover where it will and will not grow, when it crops and the pests to watch in your part of Australia; the notes here apply anywhere you can keep it warm.

sections.heading: Wax apple, rose apple or Malay apple?
sections.body: Wax jambu (Syzygium samarangense) goes by many names: wax apple, Java apple, bell fruit, jambu air, and in the shops sometimes simply rose apple. It belongs to a knot of closely related Syzygium species that are sold under overlapping names, so it pays to know what you are buying. True wax jambu has a glossy, waxy, bell-shaped fruit with crisp, watery, very mildly sweet white flesh and few or no seeds. Its close cousins are the water apple (Syzygium aqueum), the rose apple (Syzygium jambos, a smaller rose-scented fruit with a hollow seed cavity) and the Malay apple (Syzygium malaccense, a taller tree with larger red pear-shaped fruit). All four are grown much the same way, and all sit in the myrtle family alongside guava, lilly pilly and jaboticaba. If a label just says rose apple or jambu, check the botanical name or ask the nursery, because fruit size and eating quality differ between them.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Wax jambu is sold both as colour-named forms (pink, red, green, white and deep purple) and as named selections, and as a rule the darker and redder the skin, the sweeter the fruit. Most of the best modern cultivars come from Taiwan and Thailand, where wax apple is a major orchard crop. The pink-fleshed Thai selection Thabthim Chan, sold here as a pink or red type, has long, glossy crimson fruit and a long season; the prized dark selections are nicknamed Black Pearl or Black Diamond, the cheaper pale ones Green Pearl, and large green selections such as Giant Green and the Thai green Khiew Savoey are crisp and mild. Many improved selections carry few or no seeds, which is what a nursery means by a seedless wax apple, though seediness varies with the season and the tree. Whatever the colour, buy a named, clonally grown plant rather than a seedling so you know what you are getting.

sections.heading: Pollination: one tree will fruit
sections.body: You only need one wax jambu. The flowers are self-compatible and a single tree will set fruit on its own; the showy, brush-like blossoms are worked by bees, and the tree can even set some seedless fruit without fertilisation at all. What wax jambu can be fussy about is flowering in the first place: it tends to bloom after a warm, slightly drier spell, and commercial growers resort to tricks such as shading, girdling or briefly withholding water to force a reliable flowering. For a backyard tree, getting it to flower well matters far more than having a second tree for pollination.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Plant wax jambu in full sun, or light shade in the hottest districts, in fertile, free-draining soil with plenty of organic matter. It is naturally a tree of moist ground and heavier soils, and Morton notes that the soil must be fertile or the crop is small and the fruit poor, so on sand dig in compost, mulch thickly and feed more often. Give it a warm, sheltered, frost-free position, because wind tears the large soft leaves and cold checks its growth. It makes a small, wide-crowned tree of about five to fifteen metres that takes pruning well, and compact, bushy types grow happily in a large pot. Wax jambu prefers a slightly acid to neutral soil, about pH 6 to 7.5, and you can plant a container-grown tree at most times of year as long as you keep the water up (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Wax jambu is thirsty and not at all drought-hardy. In its homeland it is often planted along streams and ponds, and it wants a reliable supply of water all year, especially while the fruit is swelling; keep the soil evenly moist and mulch well, because irregular watering makes the thin skin split. For feeding, use a complete or compound fertiliser plus organic matter such as well-rotted manure or compost. The most useful rule is about timing rather than numbers: feed after each harvest to rebuild the tree, feed again as the flower buds appear, and go easy on nitrogen just before flowering, because a flush of soft leafy growth competes with the flowers and costs you fruit. Hard rates are scarce in the literature; the one detailed commercial guide, from Taiwan, gives a mature tree (over seven years old) on the order of 1.2 to 1.6 kilograms each of nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium a year, in a roughly balanced ratio rather than a high-potassium one, plus generous organic matter, with lighter and more frequent doses on sandy soils. Scale that right down for a young backyard tree and feed little and often through the warm season.

sections.heading: Harvest and ripening
sections.body: In the warm tropics wax jambu crops two or three times a year, and the fruit ripens fast, only about thirty to forty days after flowering, so a crop comes on quickly once the tree blooms. A well-grown tree comes into bearing within its first few years and a five year old tree may carry hundreds of fruit. Pick when the fruit has coloured fully and taken on its shiny, waxy sheen, because like other Syzygium it is non-climacteric: it will not ripen or sweeten any further once it leaves the tree, so picking early just gives you bland fruit. Each flush tends to ripen together over a week or two, so plan to pick every few days.

sections.heading: Eating and storing the fruit
sections.body: Wax jambu is best eaten fresh and crisp, straight from the tree or chilled, and across Southeast Asia it is dipped in salt or a little chilli and sugar, tossed through fruit salads (rujak) or pickled (asinan). More than four fifths of the fruit is edible, with only the small core to remove. The catch is that it does not keep: the thin skin bruises easily, and even refrigerated the fruit holds for only a handful of days (the Northern Territory puts its shelf life at four to six days), so it travels poorly and rarely reaches the shops. That short life is exactly why a backyard tree is worth having, since you can pick it at its crisp, juicy best.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, clonally grown wax jambu (grafted, budded or air layered, which growers call a marcot) rather than an unnamed seedling. Wax jambu does not reliably come true from seed, and seedlings take longer to bear and can throw small, seedy or bland fruit, while a marcotted or grafted plant of a known selection fruits sooner and to type. Check the botanical name on the label so you know whether you are getting true wax jambu (Syzygium samarangense) or one of its cousins, and check the colour or selection. The in-stock table above shows the named plants available right now, their pot sizes and prices.

sections.heading: Pests and protecting the fruit
sections.body: The thin, sweet skin that makes wax jambu so pleasant to eat also makes it a magnet for fruit fly, which is the main pest wherever the tree is grown; the standard answer is to bag the fruit bunches or net the tree, use baits, and clear up any fallen fruit. In warm, wet weather watch for anthracnose, a fungus that spots the leaves and rots the fruit, and for sap-sucking thrips and scale, whose honeydew brings sooty mould. The particular fruit fly you face, and the risk from myrtle rust, depend on your state, so see the section for your part of Australia below.

faqs.q: How much does a wax jambu tree cost?
faqs.a: It depends on the size of the plant and the selection. Small grafted or potted trees are usually the most affordable, while large advanced specimens and the sought-after dark selections cost more. The in-stock table on this page shows the named plants for sale right now and their current prices across the nurseries we track, so you can compare before you buy.

faqs.q: Can I grow wax jambu in a pot or a small garden?
faqs.a: Yes, within reason. Compact, bushy selections do well in a large container, which also lets you move the tree into a warm corner or under cover in a cool district. A potted specimen needs frequent watering because it dries out fast, regular feeding, and repotting or top-dressing every couple of years, and it will carry lighter crops than a tree in open ground that can root into a steady supply of moisture.

faqs.q: How long until a wax jambu tree bears fruit?
faqs.a: A named selection on a clonally grown plant usually starts cropping about three to five years after you plant it. A tree raised from seed is slower, often five to six years, and the fruit it bears is less predictable, which is the main reason to pay for a named plant rather than a cheap seedling.

faqs.q: Why is my wax jambu flowering but not setting fruit?
faqs.a: A few things commonly cause it. Cool nights and cold snaps drop the blossom, so a tree on the cool edge of its range often blooms but holds nothing. A heavy dose of nitrogen, or a surge of soft new leaves, pulls the plant's energy away from the flowers. Dry spells at flowering and too few bees also cut the result. Give it warmth, even moisture, light feeding and room for bees, and keep high-nitrogen fertiliser away until after it has flowered.

faqs.q: What does wax jambu taste like?
faqs.a: Mild, crisp and refreshing rather than rich. The flesh is watery and spongy with a light, gently sweet, faintly floral flavour that many people compare to the crunch and juice of watermelon rind or a very mild apple. The deep red and pink selections are the sweetest, while pale green ones are crisper and more delicate. It is the texture and juiciness, more than sugar, that people enjoy, which is why it is so often eaten chilled or with a dusting of salt.

WA.intro: Growing wax jambu in Western Australia. The honest answer is that wax jambu is a tropical tree, so in Western Australia its real home is the far north: the Kimberley, not the Mediterranean south-west, though keen gardeners do coax it along in warm, sheltered spots further south.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Wax jambu wants tropical heat, humidity and freedom from frost. The south-west of Western Australia has the wrong climate for it, with cool, wet winters and hot, dry summers, so it is marginal around Perth and the south. The tropical north is a different story: hot and frost-free, it gives the tree what it needs. In between, the warm but dry districts can grow it with irrigation and shelter.

sections.heading: Where wax jambu grows in WA
sections.body: The Kimberley is the state's tropical fruit bowl. The Ord River irrigation area around Kununurra, served by the Frank Wise Institute of Tropical Agriculture, grows mangoes, bananas and other tropical fruit, and its hot, frost-free climate suits wax jambu where irrigation makes up for the dry season; Broome and the wider Kimberley are similar. Carnarvon and the Gascoyne are warm and frost-free but semi-arid and subtropical rather than humid-tropical, so wax jambu is experimental there. In a sheltered, warm Perth garden it can be grown and will fruit, but it is well outside its comfort zone and crops are light.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: In the Kimberley wax jambu crops through the hot months, much as the tree does across the tropical north. In a sheltered Perth garden, if it sets at all, expect only a light crop in the warmth of summer rather than the repeated flushes a tropical tree gives.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: Western Australia's strict quarantine makes this the hard part. Live plants from other states may enter only if the species is permitted on the Western Australian Organism List, and they usually need chemical treatment and a plant health certificate first, so many eastern nurseries simply will not post wax jambu, or other plants, to Western Australia. The in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship here, which in practice means buying from a Western Australian grower. Check that a seller can send to your state before you order.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: Western Australia has its own fruit fly, the Mediterranean fruit fly, rather than the Queensland fruit fly of the eastern states, and the thin, sweet skin of wax jambu makes it an easy target, so bag or net the fruit. Medfly is found from Perth north through much of the state but is notably absent from the Ord River irrigation area around Kununurra. The better news is myrtle rust, the myrtle-family fungus established in the eastern states, has been found only once in a remote part of northern Western Australia, so the state is essentially free of it; it is a notifiable declared pest, so report any suspected case.

faqs.q: Is wax jambu worth trying in a Perth backyard?
faqs.a: It is worth a try if you have the right spot and modest expectations. Give it the warmest, most sheltered, sun-trapped corner you have, ideally against a north-facing wall, protect it from cold and wind, and keep the water up in summer; a large pot you can move under cover for winter is often the safest approach. In the south-west's Mediterranean climate it will never crop like a tree in the tropics, but local rare-fruit growers do pick fruit from sheltered trees.

faqs.q: Can I bring a wax jambu plant into Western Australia myself?
faqs.a: Only in small numbers and under strict conditions. As an individual you may carry in a limited quantity of plants for your own use, but each must be an allowed kind, washed completely clean of all soil and potting mix, and some still need treatment. For most people it is simpler and safer to buy a plant from a Western Australian nursery that already grows it, and to check the current rules with the state quarantine service first.

QLD.intro: Growing wax jambu in Queensland. Queensland is the heart of wax jambu country in Australia: it is a true lowland tropical tree, and the warm, humid far north suits it better than anywhere else in the country.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Wax jambu wants heat and humidity and no frost. It grows best where daytime temperatures sit around 23 to 28 degrees and never drops below about 7 degrees, which is why the humid coastal lowlands of the far north suit it and the cooler, higher country does not. A short, slightly drier spell helps trigger flowering, but the tree still needs reliable water through the year. In Queensland it is cold nights, not heat, that set the limit.

sections.heading: Where wax jambu grows in QLD
sections.body: The wet tropics of Far North Queensland, around Cairns, Innisfail and Tully and up the coastal strip toward the Daintree, are the most reliable place to grow it, with the heat and high rainfall the tree loves. On the cooler Atherton Tableland, around Mareeba and Atherton, the higher, cooler nights make it more marginal. The other Australian stronghold is the Northern Territory, where Darwin and the Top End grow the wax jambu group as a local market fruit, picked from about July to October.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: In the tropical north wax jambu can flower and fruit two or three times a year rather than in one fixed season, each flush ripening about a month after the bloom, so fruit can appear across the warmer part of the year. Further south and inland the crop narrows toward a single warm-season pick. In the Northern Territory the main harvest runs from July to October.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is endemic across eastern Australia and wax jambu is a listed host, so the soft, thin-skinned fruit will be stung unless you bag the bunches, net the tree, use baits and clean up fallen fruit. Myrtle rust, a fungus of the myrtle family to which wax jambu belongs, is widespread in coastal Queensland and can spot and distort the soft new growth, so keep an eye on fresh flushes. Anthracnose is the other warm, wet-weather risk.

faqs.q: Can I grow wax jambu on the Atherton Tableland, or only on the coast?
faqs.a: You can grow it up on the Tableland, but choose the spot with care. That higher, inland country has chillier nights than the coast, and wax jambu resents the cold, so a warm, sheltered, frost-free position close to the house or among other trees gives the best result, and keeping the plant small enough to cover helps on a cold night. Down on the warm coastal lowlands it is far less fussy.

faqs.q: Does wax jambu grow in Brisbane and the south-east?
faqs.a: It can, but Brisbane sits near the cool southern edge of its comfort zone, so expect a lighter, later crop than the tropical north, choose a warm, frost-free, north-facing position, and protect a young plant in a cold snap. Many south-east growers keep it in a tub so it can be wheeled somewhere sheltered for the coldest weeks.

NSW.intro: Growing wax jambu in New South Wales. New South Wales is the southern limit for wax jambu in the open: it is dependable only in the frost-free, subtropical far north of the state.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Wax jambu needs warmth and hates frost, so in New South Wales it is happy only in the warm, frost-free pockets of the far north coast. It tolerates nothing much below about 7 degrees for long, and a cold winter sets it back badly, so even around the Hunter and further south it tends to sulk and spend spring just recovering. Inland and southern districts are simply too cold for it in the open.

sections.heading: Where wax jambu grows in NSW
sections.body: The dependable area is the subtropical Northern Rivers, around the Tweed, Lismore and the far-north coast, where winters stay mild and frost is rare. South of there, along the warmer coastal fringe, it can be grown in sheltered, frost-free gardens and warm microclimates, but it becomes a gamble. It is not a tree for the tablelands or the cold inland.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: In northern New South Wales wax jambu usually gives a single main crop through the warm months of summer and into early autumn, rather than the two or three flushes it manages in the tropics. The cooler conditions mean it ripens later, and more lightly, than it does in Queensland.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly, despite its name, is native to north-eastern New South Wales and is the fruit fly to manage; bag the fruit or net the tree and clear up windfalls. Myrtle rust is established in eastern Australia, and because wax jambu is a myrtle-family plant its soft new growth can be infected, so watch the fresh flushes; in the wetter coastal north, anthracnose can also spot the leaves and rot the fruit.

faqs.q: How far south in New South Wales can I grow wax jambu?
faqs.a: Reliably, only as far south as the frost-free Northern Rivers. Along the warmer coastal strip below that you can try it in a sun-trapped, frost-free spot or a large tub, but the crop gets lighter and less certain the further south and the further inland you go. Around Sydney and colder it is a shelter-and-pot gamble rather than a garden tree.

faqs.q: Can wax jambu be posted to me in New South Wales?
faqs.a: Yes. Unlike Western Australia, New South Wales has no special quarantine barrier to wax jambu, so the eastern-state nurseries that grow it can send a plant to you. The in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that ship to your state, with their prices, so you can order one in.

VIC.intro: Growing wax jambu in Victoria. Victoria is too cold for wax jambu in the open. It is a frost-tender tropical tree, so here it is a plant for a glasshouse, a warm conservatory, or a pot that comes inside for winter.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Wax jambu cannot take frost and is checked by cold well above freezing, struggling once the temperature falls toward 7 degrees and dying at around 0 degrees. Victoria's cold, frosty winters are far outside what it can take outdoors, so it will not crop reliably in the open anywhere in the state, much as a mango or lychee will not.

sections.heading: Where wax jambu grows in VIC
sections.body: Outdoors, effectively nowhere as a fruiting tree. The only practical way to grow it in Victoria is under cover: a heated glasshouse or a bright, warm conservatory, or a large pot kept in the warmest, most sheltered Melbourne microclimate and brought inside over winter. Even then, fruit is a bonus rather than an expectation.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: There is no outdoor harvest to speak of in Victoria. Under glass, with enough warmth and light, a potted tree might set a light crop in the heat of summer, but most Victorian growers keep it for the handsome foliage and the novelty rather than for fruit.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: Cold, not pests, is what stops wax jambu in Victoria. For the record, Queensland fruit fly is present in parts of the state, including the Sunraysia district around Mildura, and myrtle rust occurs in eastern Australia, but neither matters much while the limiting factor is simply keeping the tree warm enough to live.

faqs.q: What is the best way to grow wax jambu in a cold climate like Victoria?
faqs.a: Treat it as a tender container plant. Grow it in a large pot of free-draining mix, stand it in the brightest, warmest place you have through the warm months, and move it into a heated glasshouse, a sunroom or indoors by a sunny window before the cold arrives. Keep it warm, humid and out of frost over winter, ease back the water while it is resting, and accept that fruit is unlikely; it is grown here more as a curiosity than a crop.

faqs.q: Is there a hardier alternative to wax jambu for Victoria?
faqs.a: If you want a myrtle-family fruit that copes with Victorian cold, look at feijoa, which is genuinely hardy, or lilly pilly for edible berries; jaboticaba is hardier than wax jambu, though it still fruits best with warmth. These give you the same family in a form that will actually crop outdoors across much of Victoria.
